description:
You are acting as an incident responder who has just copied an
    authentication log into the working directory for triage.

    1.  Source data  
        • File path : /home/user/data/auth.log  
        • Delimiter : vertical bar “|”  
        • Column order inside every line (exactly four columns):
              1) timestamp   – ISO-8601   e.g. 2023-05-14T08:14:22Z  
              2) user        – account id e.g. admin  
              3) ip          – remote IPv4 e.g. 192.168.1.45  
              4) event       – keyword     e.g. FAILED_LOGIN  

        The file already exists and contains six lines:

            2023-05-14T08:14:22Z|admin|192.168.1.45|FAILED_LOGIN
            2023-05-14T08:14:26Z|john|192.168.1.23|SUCCESSFUL_LOGIN
            2023-05-14T08:15:02Z|root|10.0.0.15|FAILED_LOGIN
            2023-05-14T08:15:45Z|mary|192.168.1.23|SESSION_CLOSED
            2023-05-14T08:16:03Z|test|172.16.0.8|FAILED_LOGIN
            2023-05-14T08:16:48Z|john|192.168.1.23|SUDO_SESSION_OPENED

    2.  Objective  
        Isolate every incident whose event field is exactly the
        string “FAILED_LOGIN” (case-sensitive) and prepare a short
        CSV report that can be handed to management.

    3.  Required output  
        • Create directory (if it does not yet exist):
              /home/user/report
        • Create file:
              /home/user/report/suspicious_activity.csv

          The file **must match the following format exactly**:

              a) Plain UTF-8 text.  
              b) Unix line-feeds (LF, “\n”), no CR characters.  
              c) No trailing spaces or blank lines.  
              d) Comma-separated values, no quotation marks.  
              e) Exactly four lines total:
                     Line-1  header: “timestamp,ip”
                     Line-2  “2023-05-14T08:14:22Z,192.168.1.45”
                     Line-3  “2023-05-14T08:15:02Z,10.0.0.15”
                     Line-4  “2023-05-14T08:16:03Z,172.16.0.8”

    4.  Constraints  
        • Only standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities may be
          used (grep, cut, paste, sort, etc.).  
        • Root access is unavailable, so stay under /home/user.  
        • The task is intentionally solvable with a **simple
          3-to-4-command pipeline** that makes use of “cut” and
          “paste” for column manipulation.

    5.  Deliverable verification  
        Automated tests will read
        /home/user/report/suspicious_activity.csv and compare it
        byte-for-byte against the format described in Section 3.  
        If the file path, number of lines, newline style, header
        name, or any single character differs, the check will fail.

    Your job is to work at a Linux shell prompt, inspect the source
    log, use cut-and-paste style column manipulation, and write the
    required CSV.
truth:
Pre-existing file (before the agent starts):
        Path    : /home/user/data/auth.log
        Content :
2023-05-14T08:14:22Z|admin|192.168.1.45|FAILED_LOGIN
2023-05-14T08:14:26Z|john|192.168.1.23|SUCCESSFUL_LOGIN
2023-05-14T08:15:02Z|root|10.0.0.15|FAILED_LOGIN
2023-05-14T08:15:45Z|mary|192.168.1.23|SESSION_CLOSED
2023-05-14T08:16:03Z|test|172.16.0.8|FAILED_LOGIN
2023-05-14T08:16:48Z|john|192.168.1.23|SUDO_SESSION_OPENED

    Expected file after successful completion:
        Path    : /home/user/report/suspicious_activity.csv
        Content :
timestamp,ip
2023-05-14T08:14:22Z,192.168.1.45
2023-05-14T08:15:02Z,10.0.0.15
2023-05-14T08:16:03Z,172.16.0.8

    (File ends with a single LF after the last line.)
